content.problemStatement:
You are given an array AAA of length NNN.

You are allowed to perform the following operation any number of times (possibly 000):


Select an index i (1≤i<N)i \ (1 \leq i \lt N)i (1≤i<N) and replace Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1​ with gcd⁡(Ai,Ai+1)\gcd(A_i, A_{i+1})gcd(Ai​,Ai+1​).
Is it possible to sort AAA in non-decreasing order after performing the above operation some number of times?


Input Format

The first line of input contains a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the length of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1,A_2,\ldots,A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ representing the array AAA.




Output Format
For each test case, print on a new line the answer: `YES` if it is possible to sort AAA, and `NO` otherwise.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `Yes`, `YES, `yes`, `yEs` will all be treated as identical.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case consists of two lines: the first line contains an integer N, the length of the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers A1, A2, …, AN.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to sort the array using the allowed operation, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Test case 1: array is already sorted. Test case 2: replace A2 with gcd(1,3)=1 obtaining [1,1,2] which is sorted. Test case 3: no sequence of operations can achieve a sorted order.

solution.approach: Let B be the sorted version of the original array A and let mn be the minimum element of A. The operation can only decrease values (gcd is never larger than its arguments) and it can propagate the minimum value to the right as long as the minimum divides the element it meets. Therefore an element that is already in its correct position needs no change, while an element that is out of place must be reduced to the value it has in B. This is possible iff the target value B[i] is a multiple of mn. Consequently, after sorting we simply check every index i where A[i] != B[i]; if any such B[i] is not divisible by mn the answer is NO, otherwise YES. The algorithm runs in O(N log N) per test case because of sorting, and O(N) additional memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N log N) per test case (dominated by sorting). The total over all test cases is O((sum N) log N).
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) for storing the copy of the array and its sorted version.

code.Python3:
import sys

def solve():
    data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
    if not data:
        return
    it = iter(data)
    T = int(next(it))
    out_lines = []
    for _ in range(T):
        N = int(next(it))
        a = [int(next(it)) for _ in range(N)]
        mn = min(a)
        b = sorted(a)
        ok = True
        for ai, bi in zip(a, b):
            if ai != bi and bi % mn != 0:
                ok = False
                break
        out_lines.append('YES' if ok else 'NO')
    sys.stdout.write('\n'.join(out_lines))

if __name__ == "__main__":
    solve()
